He was a student of [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]] and was regarded as one of the "Five Scholars of Orikuchi." Nishitsunoi served as a professor at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] and received a Doctor of Literature degree.
== Biography == Masayoshi Nishitsunoi was born in [[Kitaadachi District]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the village of Takahana, part of what is now [[Omiya Ward]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]]. He graduated from Saitama Middle School (now [[Saitama Prefectural Fudooka High School]]) in 1918 and completed his studies at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]]'s Department of Japanese Literature in 1922.<ref name="abe">{{harvnb|Abe Masamichi|1989}}</ref>
After graduation, he became a teacher at [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa Girls' High School]] (now [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa First Girls' High School]])<ref name="abe"/>.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/937375/155 中等教育諸学校職員録 大正11th year 5月現在 第20版, 中等教科書協会, November 1922, p.208]</ref> He later joined the teaching staff at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]]'s Shrine Priest Training Department in 1926 and became a lecturer in 1928. By 1935, he was appointed as a full professor. Between 1954 and 1958, he served as Dean of the Faculty of Letters. In 1948, Nishitsunoi submitted his dissertation, "Elements of Repose in the History of Japanese Performing Arts," to Kokugakuin University, earning him a Doctor of Literature degree.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500000491198|title=書誌事項（CiNii Dissertations）|publisher=National Institute of Informatics|accessdate=2017-03-02}}</ref>
Nishitsunoi was also active in international religious studies, serving on the Japanese Committee of the Asia-Africa Group of the [[International Association for the History of Religions]] in 1959. In 1960, he became president of the [[Shinto Theology Association]] and later served as Director of the [[Orikuchi Memorial Institute for Ancient Studies]] in 1965.
== Awards and Honors == * 1967: Recipient of the [[:en:Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon|Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon]].
== Family == The Nishitsunoi family served as hereditary priests of the [[Hikawa Shrine (Omiya)]], an influential [[shrine family]] responsible for distributing [[:en:Jingū Taima|taima]] in Saitama Prefecture.<ref name="abe"/> His father, Masao Nishitsunoi, graduated from Kokugakuin University and served as a teacher at Saitama Normal School. He also held the position of mayor of Omiya and later served as a priest at Tamaki Shrine in [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].<ref name="abe"/> His mother, Sadoko, was the elder sister of [[Kono Shozo]], a former president of Kokugakuin University, and a classmate of Empress Teimei at the [[Women's Gakushuin]]. Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at [[:en:Jissen Women's University|Jissen Women's University]]. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at [[:en:Rikkyo University|Rikkyo University]] and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]] since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.
== Publications == === Single-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽研究 (Kagura Studies) |date=1934-05 |publisher=壬生書院 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽歌研究 (Kagura Song Studies) |date=1941-05 |publisher=畝傍書房 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=村の遊び (Village Plays) |date=1943-05 |publisher=三国書房 |series=女性叢書 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=祭祀概論 (Introduction to Rituals) |date=1957-10 |publisher=[[Shinto Shimbunsha]] }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=古代祭祀と文学 (Ancient Rituals and Literature) |date=1966-09 |publisher=[[Chuokoron-sha]] }}
=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
=== Co-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |author1=[[Takasaki Masahide]] |author2=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi |title=民衆歌人 (People's Poets) |date=1936-05 |publisher=非凡閣 |series=作者別万葉集評釈 }}
== References == * [[Nagano Tsuneyuki]], "Scholar Profiles," ''Interpretation and Appreciation,'' Vol. 29, No. 14, December 1964. * "Biography of Nishitsunoi Masayoshi," ''Shinto Religion,'' Nos. 65-66, January 1972. * {{Cite Journal|和書|author=[[Abe Masamichi]]|title=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi|journal=芸能|volume=31|issue=10|year=1989|pages=24-26|naid=40000967004|ref=harv}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1900|5|22}}|death_date={{death date|1970|1|22}}|birth_place={{ill|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}|awards={{ill|Medal with Purple Ribbon|WD=Q11605748}}|native_name=西角井正慶|given_name={{ill|Masayoshi|WD=Q6782780}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11629502]] Wikidata name: Masayoshi Nishitsunoi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox academic | name = Nishitsunoi Masayoshi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1900|5|22}} | birth_place = {{JPN}}[[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], [[Kitaadachi District]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1971|1|22|1900|5|22}} | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = [[:en:Folklore studies|Folklore studies]] |alma_mater = [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] |workplaces = [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] |degree = Doctor of Literature | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }} '''Nishitsunoi Masayoshi''' (西角井 正慶, May 22, 1900 – January 22, 1971) was a Japanese scholar in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], [[:en:Folklore studies|Folklore studies]], and a [[:en:Poet|Poet]] who wrote under the pen name '''Minuma Fuyuo''' (見沼 冬男). He was a student of [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]] and was regarded as one of the "Five Scholars of Orikuchi." Nishitsunoi served as a professor at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]] and received a Doctor of Literature degree.
== Biography == Masayoshi Nishitsunoi was born in [[Kitaadachi District]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the village of Takahana, part of what is now [[Omiya Ward]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]]. He graduated from Saitama Middle School (now [[Saitama Prefectural Fudooka High School]]) in 1918 and completed his studies at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]]'s Department of Japanese Literature in 1922.<ref name="abe">{{harvnb|Abe Masamichi|1989}}</ref>
After graduation, he became a teacher at [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa Girls' High School]] (now [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa First Girls' High School]])<ref name="abe"/>.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/937375/155 中等教育諸学校職員録 大正11th year 5月現在 第20版, 中等教科書協会, November 1922, p.208]</ref> He later joined the teaching staff at [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]]'s Shrine Priest Training Department in 1926 and became a lecturer in 1928. By 1935, he was appointed as a full professor. Between 1954 and 1958, he served as Dean of the Faculty of Letters. In 1948, Nishitsunoi submitted his dissertation, "Elements of Repose in the History of Japanese Performing Arts," to Kokugakuin University, earning him a Doctor of Literature degree.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500000491198|title=書誌事項（CiNii Dissertations）|publisher=National Institute of Informatics|accessdate=2017-03-02}}</ref>
Nishitsunoi was also active in international religious studies, serving on the Japanese Committee of the Asia-Africa Group of the [[International Association for the History of Religions]] in 1959. In 1960, he became president of the [[Shinto Theology Association]] and later served as Director of the [[Orikuchi Memorial Institute for Ancient Studies]] in 1965.
== Awards and Honors == * 1967: Recipient of the [[:en:Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon|Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon]].
== Family == The Nishitsunoi family served as hereditary priests of the [[Hikawa Shrine (Omiya)]], an influential [[shrine family]] responsible for distributing [[:en:Jingū Taima|taima]] in Saitama Prefecture.<ref name="abe"/> His father, Masao Nishitsunoi, graduated from Kokugakuin University and served as a teacher at Saitama Normal School. He also held the position of mayor of Omiya and later served as a priest at Tamaki Shrine in [[:en:Kazusa Province|Kazusa Province]].<ref name="abe"/> His mother, Sadoko, was the elder sister of [[Kono Shozo]], a former president of Kokugakuin University, and a classmate of Empress Teimei at the [[Women's Gakushuin]]. Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at [[:en:Jissen Women's University|Jissen Women's University]]. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at [[:en:Rikkyo University|Rikkyo University]] and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]] since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.
== Publications == === Single-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽研究 (Kagura Studies) |date=1934-05 |publisher=壬生書院 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽歌研究 (Kagura Song Studies) |date=1941-05 |publisher=畝傍書房 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=村の遊び (Village Plays) |date=1943-05 |publisher=三国書房 |series=女性叢書 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=祭祀概論 (Introduction to Rituals) |date=1957-10 |publisher=[[Shinto Shimbunsha]] }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=古代祭祀と文学 (Ancient Rituals and Literature) |date=1966-09 |publisher=[[Chuokoron-sha]] }}
=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
=== Co-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |author1=[[Takasaki Masahide]] |author2=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi |title=民衆歌人 (People's Poets) |date=1936-05 |publisher=非凡閣 |series=作者別万葉集評釈 }}
== References == * [[Nagano Tsuneyuki]], "Scholar Profiles," ''Interpretation and Appreciation,'' Vol. 29, No. 14, December 1964. * "Biography of Nishitsunoi Masayoshi," ''Shinto Religion,'' Nos. 65-66, January 1972. * {{Cite Journal|和書|author=[[Abe Masamichi]]|title=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi|journal=芸能|volume=31|issue=10|year=1989|pages=24-26|naid=40000967004|ref=harv}}

Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at {{ill|Jissen Women's University|en|Jissen Women's University|WD=Q6203214|lt=Jissen Women's University}}. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at {{ill|Rikkyo University|en|Rikkyo University|WD=Q1143235|lt=Rikkyo University}} and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a {{ill|Shinto priest|en|Shinto priest|WD=Q60523|lt=Shinto priest}} since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.
== Publications == === Single-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽研究 (Kagura Studies) |date=1934-05 |publisher=壬生書院 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽歌研究 (Kagura Song Studies) |date=1941-05 |publisher=畝傍書房 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=村の遊び (Village Plays) |date=1943-05 |publisher=三国書房 |series=女性叢書 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=祭祀概論 (Introduction to Rituals) |date=1957-10 |publisher=[[Shinto Shimbunsha]] }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=古代祭祀と文学 (Ancient Rituals and Literature) |date=1966-09 |publisher=[[Chuokoron-sha]] }}
=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
=== Co-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |author1=[[Takasaki Masahide]] |author2=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi |title=民衆歌人 (People's Poets) |date=1936-05 |publisher=非凡閣 |series=作者別万葉集評釈 }}
== References == * [[Nagano Tsuneyuki]], "Scholar Profiles," ''Interpretation and Appreciation,'' Vol. 29, No. 14, December 1964. * "Biography of Nishitsunoi Masayoshi," ''Shinto Religion,'' Nos. 65-66, January 1972. * {{Cite Journal|和書|author=[[Abe Masamichi]]|title=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi|journal=芸能|volume=31|issue=10|year=1989|pages=24-26|naid=40000967004|ref=harv}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1900|5|22}}|death_date={{death date|1970|1|22}}|birth_place={{ill|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052}}|awards={{ill|Medal with Purple Ribbon|WD=Q11605748}}|native_name=西角井正慶|given_name={{ill|Masayoshi|WD=Q6782780}}}} {{Infobox academic | name = Nishitsunoi Masayoshi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1900|5|22}} | birth_place = {{JPN}}{{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, [[Kitaadachi District]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1971|1|22|1900|5|22}} | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=Folklore studies}} |alma_mater = {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} |workplaces = {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} |degree = Doctor of Literature | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }} '''Nishitsunoi Masayoshi''' (西角井 正慶, May 22, 1900 – January 22, 1971) was a Japanese scholar in {{ill|Japanese literature|en|Japanese literature|WD=Q231425|lt=Japanese literature}}, {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=Folklore studies}}, and a {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet}} who wrote under the pen name '''Minuma Fuyuo''' (見沼 冬男). He was a student of [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]] and was regarded as one of the "Five Scholars of Orikuchi." Nishitsunoi served as a professor at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} and received a Doctor of Literature degree.
== Biography == Masayoshi Nishitsunoi was born in [[Kitaadachi District]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the village of Takahana, part of what is now [[Omiya Ward]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]]. He graduated from Saitama Middle School (now [[Saitama Prefectural Fudooka High School]]) in 1918 and completed his studies at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}}'s Department of Japanese Literature in 1922.<ref name="abe">{{harvnb|Abe Masamichi|1989}}</ref>
After graduation, he became a teacher at [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa Girls' High School]] (now [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa First Girls' High School]])<ref name="abe"/>.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/937375/155 中等教育諸学校職員録 大正11th year 5月現在 第20版, 中等教科書協会, November 1922, p.208]</ref> He later joined the teaching staff at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}}'s Shrine Priest Training Department in 1926 and became a lecturer in 1928. By 1935, he was appointed as a full professor. Between 1954 and 1958, he served as Dean of the Faculty of Letters. In 1948, Nishitsunoi submitted his dissertation, "Elements of Repose in the History of Japanese Performing Arts," to Kokugakuin University, earning him a Doctor of Literature degree.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500000491198|title=書誌事項（CiNii Dissertations）|publisher=National Institute of Informatics|accessdate=2017-03-02}}</ref>
Nishitsunoi was also active in international religious studies, serving on the Japanese Committee of the Asia-Africa Group of the [[International Association for the History of Religions]] in 1959. In 1960, he became president of the [[Shinto Theology Association]] and later served as Director of the [[Orikuchi Memorial Institute for Ancient Studies]] in 1965.
== Awards and Honors == * 1967: Recipient of the [[:en:Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon|Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon]].
== Family == The Nishitsunoi family served as hereditary priests of the [[Hikawa Shrine (Omiya)]], an influential [[shrine family]] responsible for distributing [[:en:Jingū Taima|taima]] in Saitama Prefecture.<ref name="abe"/> His father, Masao Nishitsunoi, graduated from Kokugakuin University and served as a teacher at Saitama Normal School. He also held the position of mayor of Omiya and later served as a priest at Tamaki Shrine in {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|WD=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province}}.<ref name="abe"/> His mother, Sadoko, was the elder sister of [[Kono Shozo]], a former president of Kokugakuin University, and a classmate of Empress Teimei at the [[Women's Gakushuin]]. Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at {{ill|Jissen Women's University|en|Jissen Women's University|WD=Q6203214|lt=Jissen Women's University}}. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at {{ill|Rikkyo University|en|Rikkyo University|WD=Q1143235|lt=Rikkyo University}} and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a {{ill|Shinto priest|en|Shinto priest|WD=Q60523|lt=Shinto priest}} since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.
== Publications == === Single-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽研究 (Kagura Studies) |date=1934-05 |publisher=壬生書院 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽歌研究 (Kagura Song Studies) |date=1941-05 |publisher=畝傍書房 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=村の遊び (Village Plays) |date=1943-05 |publisher=三国書房 |series=女性叢書 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=祭祀概論 (Introduction to Rituals) |date=1957-10 |publisher=[[Shinto Shimbunsha]] }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=古代祭祀と文学 (Ancient Rituals and Literature) |date=1966-09 |publisher=[[Chuokoron-sha]] }}
=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
=== Co-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |author1=[[Takasaki Masahide]] |author2=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi |title=民衆歌人 (People's Poets) |date=1936-05 |publisher=非凡閣 |series=作者別万葉集評釈 }}
== References == * [[Nagano Tsuneyuki]], "Scholar Profiles," ''Interpretation and Appreciation,'' Vol. 29, No. 14, December 1964. * "Biography of Nishitsunoi Masayoshi," ''Shinto Religion,'' Nos. 65-66, January 1972. * {{Cite Journal|和書|author=[[Abe Masamichi]]|title=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi|journal=芸能|volume=31|issue=10|year=1989|pages=24-26|naid=40000967004|ref=harv}}

He was a student of [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]] and was regarded as one of the "Five Scholars of Orikuchi." Nishitsunoi served as a professor at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}} and received a Doctor of Literature degree.
== Biography == Masayoshi Nishitsunoi was born in [[Kitaadachi District]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the village of Takahana, part of what is now [[Omiya Ward]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]]. He graduated from Saitama Middle School (now [[Saitama Prefectural Fudooka High School]]) in 1918 and completed his studies at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}}'s Department of Japanese Literature in 1922.<ref name="abe">{{harvnb|Abe Masamichi|1989}}</ref>
After graduation, he became a teacher at [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa Girls' High School]] (now [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa First Girls' High School]])<ref name="abe"/>.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/937375/155 中等教育諸学校職員録 大正11th year 5月現在 第20版, 中等教科書協会, November 1922, p.208]</ref> He later joined the teaching staff at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University}}'s Shrine Priest Training Department in 1926 and became a lecturer in 1928. By 1935, he was appointed as a full professor. Between 1954 and 1958, he served as Dean of the Faculty of Letters. In 1948, Nishitsunoi submitted his dissertation, "Elements of Repose in the History of Japanese Performing Arts," to Kokugakuin University, earning him a Doctor of Literature degree.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500000491198|title=書誌事項（CiNii Dissertations）|publisher=National Institute of Informatics|accessdate=2017-03-02}}</ref>
Nishitsunoi was also active in international religious studies, serving on the Japanese Committee of the Asia-Africa Group of the [[International Association for the History of Religions]] in 1959. In 1960, he became president of the [[Shinto Theology Association]] and later served as Director of the [[Orikuchi Memorial Institute for Ancient Studies]] in 1965.
== Awards and Honors == * 1967: Recipient of the [[:en:Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon|Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon]].
== Family == The Nishitsunoi family served as hereditary priests of the [[Hikawa Shrine (Omiya)]], an influential [[shrine family]] responsible for distributing [[:en:Jingū Taima|taima]] in Saitama Prefecture.<ref name="abe"/> His father, Masao Nishitsunoi, graduated from Kokugakuin University and served as a teacher at Saitama Normal School. He also held the position of mayor of Omiya and later served as a priest at Tamaki Shrine in {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|WD=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province}}.<ref name="abe"/> His mother, Sadoko, was the elder sister of [[Kono Shozo]], a former president of Kokugakuin University, and a classmate of Empress Teimei at the [[Women's Gakushuin]]. Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at {{ill|Jissen Women's University|en|Jissen Women's University|WD=Q6203214|lt=Jissen Women's University}}. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at {{ill|Rikkyo University|en|Rikkyo University|WD=Q1143235|lt=Rikkyo University}} and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a {{ill|Shinto priest|en|Shinto priest|WD=Q60523|lt=Shinto priest}} since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.

=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
=== Co-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |author1=[[Takasaki Masahide]] |author2=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi |title=民衆歌人 (People's Poets) |date=1936-05 |publisher=非凡閣 |series=作者別万葉集評釈 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:西角井正慶]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1900|5|22}}|death_date={{death date|1970|1|22}}|birth_place={{ill|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|folklorist|WD=Q3075052|1=folklorist}}|awards={{ill|Medal with Purple Ribbon|WD=Q11605748|1=Medal with Purple Ribbon}}|native_name=西角井正慶|given_name={{ill|Masayoshi|WD=Q6782780|1=Masayoshi}}}} {{Infobox academic | name = Nishitsunoi Masayoshi | image = | image_size = | caption = | birth_date = {{birth date|1900|5|22}} | birth_place = {{JPN}}{{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, [[Kitaadachi District]] | death_date = {{death date and age|1971|1|22|1900|5|22}} | death_place = | othername = |main_interests = {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=Folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}} |alma_mater = {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}} |workplaces = {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}} |degree = Doctor of Literature | URL = | yearsactive = | spouse = | children = }} '''Nishitsunoi Masayoshi''' (西角井 正慶, May 22, 1900 – January 22, 1971) was a Japanese scholar in {{ill|Japanese literature|en|Japanese literature|WD=Q231425|lt=Japanese literature|1=Japanese literature}}, {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=Folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}}, and a {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet|1=Poet}} who wrote under the pen name '''Minuma Fuyuo''' (見沼 冬男). He was a student of [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]] and was regarded as one of the "Five Scholars of Orikuchi." Nishitsunoi served as a professor at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}} and received a Doctor of Literature degree.
== Biography == Masayoshi Nishitsunoi was born in [[Kitaadachi District]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the village of Takahana, part of what is now [[Omiya Ward]], [[:en:Saitama City|Saitama City]]. He graduated from Saitama Middle School (now [[Saitama Prefectural Fudooka High School]]) in 1918 and completed his studies at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}}'s Department of Japanese Literature in 1922.<ref name="abe">{{harvnb|Abe Masamichi|1989}}</ref>
After graduation, he became a teacher at [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa Girls' High School]] (now [[Saitama Prefectural Urawa First Girls' High School]])<ref name="abe"/>.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/937375/155 中等教育諸学校職員録 大正11th year 5月現在 第20版, 中等教科書協会, November 1922, p.208]</ref> He later joined the teaching staff at {{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|WD=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}}'s Shrine Priest Training Department in 1926 and became a lecturer in 1928. By 1935, he was appointed as a full professor. Between 1954 and 1958, he served as Dean of the Faculty of Letters. In 1948, Nishitsunoi submitted his dissertation, "Elements of Repose in the History of Japanese Performing Arts," to Kokugakuin University, earning him a Doctor of Literature degree.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/500000491198|title=書誌事項（CiNii Dissertations）|publisher=National Institute of Informatics|accessdate=2017-03-02}}</ref>
Nishitsunoi was also active in international religious studies, serving on the Japanese Committee of the Asia-Africa Group of the [[International Association for the History of Religions]] in 1959. In 1960, he became president of the [[Shinto Theology Association]] and later served as Director of the [[Orikuchi Memorial Institute for Ancient Studies]] in 1965.
== Awards and Honors == * 1967: Recipient of the [[:en:Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon|Medal of Honor with Purple Ribbon]].
== Family == The Nishitsunoi family served as hereditary priests of the [[Hikawa Shrine (Omiya)]], an influential [[shrine family]] responsible for distributing [[:en:Jingū Taima|taima]] in Saitama Prefecture.<ref name="abe"/> His father, Masao Nishitsunoi, graduated from Kokugakuin University and served as a teacher at Saitama Normal School. He also held the position of mayor of Omiya and later served as a priest at Tamaki Shrine in {{ill|Kazusa Province|en|Kazusa Province|WD=Q1055394|lt=Kazusa Province|1=Kazusa Province}}.<ref name="abe"/> His mother, Sadoko, was the elder sister of [[Kono Shozo]], a former president of Kokugakuin University, and a classmate of Empress Teimei at the [[Women's Gakushuin]]. Nishitsunoi was praised by Orikuchi Shinobu as "a man born under a lucky star."
== Anecdotes == {{Citation needed|date=October 2019|section=1}} * Nishitsunoi was affectionately called "Kaku-san." * The family tradition of using the kanji "正" (Masao, Masayoshi) was continued by his sons: Masahiro (eldest), Masahiko (second), and Masafumi (third). His eldest son, Masahiro Nishitsunoi, taught folk performing arts at {{ill|Jissen Women's University|en|Jissen Women's University|WD=Q6203214|lt=Jissen Women's University|1=Jissen Women's University}}. [[Tomio Fujita]], a professor at {{ill|Rikkyo University|en|Rikkyo University|WD=Q1143235|lt=Rikkyo University|1=Rikkyo University}} and religious studies scholar, was his son-in-law. * As a child, Nishitsunoi was known to be emotional, earning the nickname "crybaby." In middle school, he excelled in short-distance running. * His aversion to raw fish influenced his decision not to become a {{ill|Shinto priest|en|Shinto priest|WD=Q60523|lt=Shinto priest|1=Kannushi}} since offerings of raw fish during shrine rituals made him ill. However, he did tolerate some cured fish such as dried sardines, salted salmon, and kusaya. Later in life, he managed to eat tuna sashimi and sea bream. * Orikuchi introduced Nishitsunoi to waka composition, and Nishitsunoi joined the Araragi poetry group under the pen name Minuma Fuyuo. While his poetic activity peaked during the Taisho era, he eventually declared Minuma Fuyuo "dead" during the war, limiting his postwar output to light-hearted poems. Nonetheless, he continued to serve as a judge for poetry offerings at the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] for many years.
== Publications == === Single-authored Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽研究 (Kagura Studies) |date=1934-05 |publisher=壬生書院 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=神楽歌研究 (Kagura Song Studies) |date=1941-05 |publisher=畝傍書房 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=村の遊び (Village Plays) |date=1943-05 |publisher=三国書房 |series=女性叢書 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=祭祀概論 (Introduction to Rituals) |date=1957-10 |publisher=[[Shinto Shimbunsha]] }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=古代祭祀と文学 (Ancient Rituals and Literature) |date=1966-09 |publisher=[[Chuokoron-sha]] }}
=== Edited Works === * {{Cite book |和書 |title=まつり (Festivals) |date=1956-01 |publisher=[[Seibundo Shinkosha]] |series=こども百科 50 }} * {{Cite book |和書 |title=年中行事辞典 (Encyclopedia of Annual Events) |date=1958-05 |publisher=[[Tokyo-do Publishing]] }}
